title: Prime Number Identification Demonstrated with Quantum Processors Using a New Rescaling-Based Noise Mitigation Technique
authors: Victor F. dos Santos, Victor P. Brasil, Pedro A. S. Contri, Jonas Maziero
abstract: We implement a quantum protocol for prime number identification based on entanglement dynamics, using IBM quantum processors. The method links the primality of an integer to specific Fourier components extracted from the time evolution of entanglement in a bipartite quantum system. To mitigate experimental noise, we introduce a noise-mitigation method based on a global rescaling factor, which is calibrated on a subset of circuits and extrapolated across different configurations. Theoretical support is provided by a new analytical bound for the Fourier modes derived assuming an initial uniform superposition state. This new bound enhances the separation between prime and composite numbers under moderate experimental deviations. These results represent a step toward practical number-theoretic applications on noisy intermediate-scale quantum (NISQ) devices.
summary: This work proposes a quantum protocol to identify prime numbers by analyzing the Fourier spectrum of entanglement dynamics in a bipartite quantum system. To make this feasible on current noisy hardware, the authors introduce and validate a rescaling-based error mitigation technique called CFE. The success of the method demonstrates a pathway for applying number theory directly to NISQ quantum processors.
main_problem: The paper addresses the challenge of performing number-theoretic computations, specifically prime number identification, using noisy quantum processors.
main_result: The authors demonstrate that the Fourier components extracted from the time evolution of entanglement purity can serve as a robust signature for primality, even when enhanced by a novel noise mitigation technique.
method: The core methodology involves linking primality to the minimal values of Fourier modes derived from the purity ($\gamma_A(t)$) of a bipartite quantum system, while employing Correction Factor Extrapolation (CFE) to correct for hardware noise.
model_or_system: The system is a bipartite quantum system AB evolving under a time-independent Hamiltonian. The protocol uses the SWAP test to measure entanglement purity, $\gamma_A(t)$, whose Fourier components are analyzed for number-theoretic properties.
key_observables: Purity of subsystem A ($\gamma_A(t)$), Fourier modes ($\alpha_n$), and the optimized correction factor ($\Lambda_{opt}$).
important_parameters: System dimensions ($d=4, 8, 16$), time evolution parameters ($\omega=0.1 	ext{s}^{-1}$), and noise scaling factors ($\xi_j$).
main_assumptions: The theoretical bounds for primality are derived assuming an initial uniform superposition state, and the CFE method requires calibration on a specific subset of circuits.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the CFE procedure, showing raw vs. mitigated purity and Fourier modes; they also plot the extrapolation of the optimal correction factor $\Lambda_{opt}(d)$ across different dimensions.
paper_structure: The paper develops the theoretical link between entanglement dynamics and primality, introduces the CFE error mitigation technique, validates this technique using numerical simulations on IBM hardware, and finally presents the refined theoretical bounds for composite numbers.
why_interesting: It provides a concrete, albeit highly complex, example of mapping fundamental number theory problems onto quantum hardware diagnostics, which is a key area for quantum algorithm development.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Schematic representation of the Correction Factor Extrapolation (CFE) procedure. The method is divided into three stages. In part A (Preparation), the target function f(λ, νi), the parameter set L, and the calibration subset Lcal are defined. In this work we set f(λ, νi) = αn, i.e., the Fourier-mode amplitude αn of the purity curve; λ = d is the system dimension and νi = n is the Fourier-mode index. In part B (Calibration), experimental data ˜f(λ, νi) are obtained for λ ∈Lcal, and the optimal correction factors Λopt(λ) are determined by minimizing the deviation function E(λ). In part C (Extrapolation and Application), the extrapolated region Lext = L \ Lcal is considered, where...

FIG. 2. Reduced purity (left column) and Fourier modes (right column) for d = 4, 8, and 16, obtained from quantum-computer implementations using p = 30, 120, and 480 time points, respectively, and 213 shots for ev- ery dimension. Each row corresponds to a different value of d, illustrating how the system’s behavior changes with increasing dimensionality. In both purity and Fourier-modes plots, the red points represent the theoretical predictions from the algorithm, the blue points correspond to the results obtained directly from the hardware implementations, and the orange points show the mitigated results after applying our post-processing technique (CFE). The previously developed analysis...

FIG. 3. Extrapolation of the correction factor Λopt(d) based on results from the implementations for d = 4, 8 and 16. The green dots represent the numerically determined optimal correction factors for the Fourier mode data obtained from the hardware implementations. The blue curve corresponds to the constructed model Λopt(d), which predicts the correction factor behavior for any dimension d ≥4. Red dots, shown in the tuple format � d, Λopt(d)  , are sampled directly from the model and lie on the blue curve.

FIG. 4. Analytical Fourier modes for d = 64, showing that the composite bound Pn may be violated after the threshold points n(2) th ≈79 and n(3) th ≈123. The brown and pink lines represent the interpolated Fourier modes corresponding to semiprimes with k = 2 and k = 3, respectively. Each of these lines eventually reaches the composite bound Pn; the larger the value of k, the higher the value of n at which this occurs. In this case, both B(2) n and B(3) n intersect Pn within the interval Nd, illustrating the coexistence of the two possible reversals of inequality (A10). The light blue region between the composite bound Pn and the lower bound Bn indicates where a divisibility test must be...

FIG. 5. Zero-noise extrapolation (ZNE) results for the PIED algorithm with d = 4. Panel (a) shows the evolu- tion of the purity of subsystem A as a function of time, obtained at two scaled noise levels and extrapolated to the zero-noise limit through linear fitting. Panel (b) displays the corresponding Fourier-mode amplitudes extracted from the mitigated data. For each point, the error bars represent the standard error of the mean over three inde- pendent batches. Specifically, for each noise-scaling factor (ξ = 1.0 and ξ = 2.0), standard errors are computed from the three batch results, and the ZNE error bars are calculated by propagating the corresponding uncertainties from the same batch...
